text:
Self-assembling molecular drugs bridge the gap between the synthetic simplicity of small-molecule chemotherapy and the superior tumor-targeting capabilities of nanoparticle conjugates. However, the successful implementation of this strategy depends on supramolecular interactions strong enough to persist within the complex physiological environment of a living animal. In this study, we demonstrate that metallophilic interactions between cyclometalated palladium complexes facilitate the formation of stable supramolecular nanostructures directly in vivo. Using a skin melanoma mouse model, we found that these self-assembled structures exhibit an extended blood circulation time exceeding 12 hours and achieve a remarkably high tumor accumulation rate of up to 10.2% of the injected dose per gram. 

Upon activation with green light, the palladium assemblies trigger a potent type I photodynamic effect, resulting in efficient tumor destruction. In vitro studies further confirmed this efficacy, showing an increase in cytotoxicity of up to 96-fold following irradiation. These findings establish that metallophilic interactions are a robust and effective tool for generating stable, self-delivering nanotherapeutics with exceptional targeting precision in vivo.
